story.title: User authentication — register, login, JWT session
story.raw_body:
## Story
As a user, I want to register and log in to the application so that I can access protected features.

## Acceptance criteria
- User can register with name, email, and password
- Password must be at least 8 characters
- User receives a JWT token on successful login
- Protected routes reject requests without a valid token
- Duplicate email registration returns a clear error

## Out of scope
- OAuth / social login
- Password reset flow (separate story)

## Notes
MERN stack. MongoDB for persistence. JWT for auth.

functional_requirements.description: A new user must be able to register by providing their name, email address, and a password.
functional_requirements.user_story: As a visitor, I want to register with my name, email, and password, so that I can create an account and access the application.

functional_requirements.description: Registration must reject passwords shorter than 8 characters with a clear validation error.
functional_requirements.user_story: As a visitor, I want to be informed when my password is too short, so that I can choose a secure password that meets the minimum requirements.

functional_requirements.description: Registration must reject duplicate email addresses and return a clear error message.
functional_requirements.user_story: As a visitor, I want to receive a clear error when I try to register with an already-used email, so that I understand why registration failed.

functional_requirements.description: A registered user must be able to log in with their email and password and receive a JWT token upon success.
functional_requirements.user_story: As a registered user, I want to log in with my email and password, so that I receive a JWT token to access protected features.

functional_requirements.description: Protected API routes must reject requests that do not include a valid JWT token, returning an appropriate error response.
functional_requirements.user_story: As a system, I want to block unauthenticated requests to protected routes, so that only authenticated users can access protected features.

non_functional_requirements.description: User passwords must be hashed using bcrypt before storage; plaintext passwords must never be persisted.
JWT tokens must be signed with a secret key stored in environment variables, never hardcoded in source.
JWT tokens must carry an expiry claim; the token lifetime must be configurable via environment variable.
All validation errors (missing fields, invalid format, duplicate email) must return HTTP 4xx responses with a machine-readable error message — never HTTP 500.
Error responses must include a human-readable message field so that the client can display meaningful feedback to the user.

criteria: Given a visitor submits a valid name, unique email, and a password of 8+ characters, When POST /api/v1/auth/register is called, Then the server responds with HTTP 201 and the new user's id, name, and email (no password).
Given a visitor omits one or more required fields (name, email, or password), When POST /api/v1/auth/register is called, Then the server responds with HTTP 400 and a descriptive validation error.

Given a visitor provides a password shorter than 8 characters, When POST /api/v1/auth/register is called, Then the server responds with HTTP 400 and an error message stating the password minimum length requirement.

Given a visitor submits an email address that already exists in the database, When POST /api/v1/auth/register is called, Then the server responds with HTTP 409 and an error message indicating the email is already registered.
Given a registered user submits their correct email and password, When POST /api/v1/auth/login is called, Then the server responds with HTTP 200 and a signed JWT token.

Given a user submits an incorrect password or an unregistered email, When POST /api/v1/auth/login is called, Then the server responds with HTTP 401 and an error message (must not reveal whether email or password is the cause).
Given a request to a protected route includes no Authorization header, When the middleware validates auth, Then the server responds with HTTP 401.
Given a request to a protected route includes a malformed or expired JWT token, When the middleware validates auth, Then the server responds with HTTP 401.

Given a request to a protected route includes a valid unexpired JWT token, When the middleware validates auth, Then the request proceeds to the route handler and returns HTTP 200 (or the appropriate success code).

entities.name: User
entities.description: Represents a registered application user with credentials.

api_endpoints_needed.purpose: Register a new user with name, email, and password.
Authenticate an existing user and return a signed JWT token.
Return the currently authenticated user's profile (validates the JWT token; serves as the canonical protected-route example).

open_questions: What should the JWT token expiry duration be? The story does not specify a value (e.g. 1 hour, 24 hours, 7 days).
Should the registration response return a JWT token immediately (auto-login), or should the user be required to log in separately after registering?
Is there a required format or complexity rule for passwords beyond the 8-character minimum (e.g. must contain uppercase, number, or special character)?
Should the /api/v1/auth/me endpoint (or another protected route) be included in this story to demonstrate JWT middleware, or is middleware-only coverage sufficient?

services.name: AuthService
services.responsibility: Encapsulates all authentication business logic including user registration, login credential verification, and JWT token generation.

methods.name: registerUser
methods.returns: Object — { id, name, email } of the newly created user (no passwordHash)
methods.description: Checks for duplicate email (throws 409 AppError if found), hashes the password with bcrypt, persists the new User document, and returns safe user fields.

methods.name: loginUser
methods.returns: Object — { token: String } containing a signed JWT
methods.description: Looks up the user by email, compares the provided password against the stored bcrypt hash (throws generic 401 AppError if either check fails to avoid enumeration), then signs and returns a JWT.

methods.name: getUserById
methods.returns: Object — { id, name, email, createdAt } (no passwordHash)
methods.description: Fetches a user document by its MongoDB _id and returns safe public fields; used by the /me endpoint after authentication middleware resolves the token.

mongoose_schema_fields.description: Full display name of the registered user
User's email address, used as the unique login identifier; stored in lowercase
bcrypt hash of the user's plaintext password; never returned in API responses
Timestamp when the user document was created; auto-managed by Mongoose timestamps option

endpoints.description: Registers a new user; returns 201 with id, name, email on success

response_errors.condition: Missing or invalid fields (name, email, password format/length)

endpoints.description: Authenticates a user with email and password; returns a signed JWT on success

response_errors.condition: Missing or invalid fields (email format, password missing)
Email not found or password does not match (generic message to prevent enumeration)

endpoints.description: Returns the authenticated user's profile; requires a valid Bearer JWT in the Authorization header

response_errors.condition: JWT token is malformed, invalid, or expired

methods.name: register
methods.description: Thin handler — extracts name/email/password from req.body, delegates to AuthService.registerUser, returns HTTP 201 with the new user object.

methods.name: login
methods.description: Thin handler — extracts email/password from req.body, delegates to AuthService.loginUser, returns HTTP 200 with the JWT token.

methods.name: getMe
methods.description: Thin handler — reads req.user.id set by authenticate middleware, delegates to AuthService.getUserById, returns HTTP 200 with the user profile.

middleware.name: authenticate
middleware.purpose: Extracts the Bearer token from the Authorization header, verifies it with jsonwebtoken using the JWT_SECRET env variable, attaches the decoded payload as req.user, and calls next(). Returns HTTP 401 via the central error handler if the token is absent, malformed, or expired.

middleware.name: validateBody
middleware.purpose: Higher-order middleware factory that accepts a Joi schema, validates req.body against it, and calls next() on success. On failure it passes a formatted 400 AppError to the central error handler with the Joi validation message.

middleware.name: errorHandler
middleware.purpose: Central Express error-handling middleware (4-argument signature). Maps AppError instances to their status code and message. Catches unexpected errors and returns HTTP 500 with a generic message so internal details are never leaked.
middleware.used_on: Globally — registered as the last middleware in server/src/index.js

env_variables_needed.purpose: TCP port the Express server listens on (default 5000)
MongoDB connection string used by Mongoose to connect to the database
Secret key used to sign and verify JWT tokens; must be kept confidential
JWT token lifetime (e.g. '1d', '7d', '1h'); passed as the expiresIn option to jsonwebtoken.sign()
Number of bcrypt salt rounds used when hashing passwords (default 10); higher values increase security at the cost of CPU time

architecture_decisions.decision: AuthService is the sole layer that imports the User model.
architecture_decisions.rationale: Enforces the CLAUDE.md convention that controllers never call models directly, keeping business logic isolated and testable without HTTP context.

architecture_decisions.decision: A generic 401 error is returned for both 'email not found' and 'wrong password' scenarios during login.
architecture_decisions.rationale: Prevents user enumeration attacks; aligns with the acceptance criterion FR-04 which explicitly states the error must not reveal which field caused the failure.

architecture_decisions.decision: Password hashing is performed inside AuthService.registerUser rather than as a Mongoose pre-save hook.
architecture_decisions.rationale: Keeps the hashing logic visible and testable at the service layer. Mongoose hooks can be accidentally bypassed (e.g. via updateOne); explicit hashing in the service is safer and more predictable.

architecture_decisions.decision: validateBody is implemented as a middleware factory (higher-order function) rather than separate middleware files per route.
architecture_decisions.rationale: Reduces file count and duplication; a single reusable factory accepts any Joi schema, making it trivial to add validation to future routes.

architecture_decisions.decision: JWT_EXPIRES_IN is an environment variable rather than a hardcoded value.
architecture_decisions.rationale: Satisfies NFR-03 (token lifetime must be configurable) and NFR-02 (no secrets hardcoded), and allows different lifetimes across environments without code changes.

architecture_decisions.decision: All environment variables are accessed through server/src/config/env.js rather than process.env directly.
architecture_decisions.rationale: Enforces the CLAUDE.md convention, centralises env-var validation at startup so the server fails fast with a clear message if required variables are missing.
